No. The Crimson Notebooks were specifically retained by Hennessy as Schedule A item #11; they were not on the 2019 intake list.

Yes. The 2022 supplementary deed amended Schedule A to remove items #1, #4, and #11; item #11 is the Crimson Notebooks. The 2022 intake receipt #ARC-2022-0247 confirms the notebooks were physically transferred on April 21, 2022.
No. The September 24, 2024 draft was never signed and is retained for procedural reference only; it is not operative. The October 12, 2024 signed codicil supersedes it.

The codicil revokes the 2022 supplementary deed of gift's conveyance of the Crimson Notebooks and bequeaths them jointly and equally to Hennessy's three children: Katherine Hennessy-Brown, Daniel Hennessy, and Sophia Hennessy-Marchetti. The codicil's validity and legal effect are pending probate court determination.
'Reading room access only; physical removal restricted pending probate court determination,' added April 30, 2026.
On the third shelf from the top, west wall, of decedent's home study at 247 Old Cobb Lane, Brookline, Massachusetts, per Photo PH-2020-067.

On April 21, 2022, the notebooks were transferred from decedent's home shelf to Hartwell Special Collections, per intake receipt #ARC-2022-0247. The shelf is photographed empty on the same date (Photo PH-2022-181).
At Hartwell College Special Collections, in the Reardon Manuscript Vault, Vault Bay 14, with reading room access only and physical removal restricted pending probate court determination.
None. As of packet date (May 4, 2026), no determinations have been issued by the court on any disputed question.
No. The estate's reply on the reservation-of-access point is dated April 28; Hartwell's reply on this point is not yet filed.

M.G.L. c. 190B § 2-502. The estate's brief argues that the codicil's witnessing by two attesting witnesses (Lawrence Petrov and Jamilah Otieno) and notarization (Notary Public Carlos Mendoza) satisfies the formal requirements.

The completed inter vivos gift theory. Hartwell's memo asserts that title to the Crimson Notebooks passed at the moment of delivery on April 21, 2022, and that a subsequent codicil cannot revoke a completed transfer of personal property.
That the 2022 supplementary deed contained a reservation of access for Hennessy's lifetime, making the gift conditional or incomplete and therefore revocable.
The Probate Court (Magistrate Judge Frances Albertine), at the May 18, 2026 evidentiary hearing.
(a) The capacity question regarding the October 12, 2024 codicil; and (b) the legal effect of the codicil if found valid.
Three items: (a) a bound manuscript of decedent's 1972 monograph corrections; (b) an album of family photographs from his 1965 trip to Japan; and (c) the three Crimson Notebooks (MS-Hennessy-068).
Three children: Katherine Hennessy-Brown, Daniel Hennessy, and Sophia Hennessy-Marchetti, jointly and equally.
No. Pending the May 18, 2026 evidentiary hearing.
No. Pending court ruling after the May 18 hearing.
No. Hartwell's reply on the reservation-of-access point is not yet filed; the question is pending.
No. Per the scheduling order, determinations on ownership and physical disposition are deferred until after the May 18 hearing.

Neither has been determined to be currently determinative; the dispute is pending. Hartwell asserts the 2022 supplementary deed is a completed inter vivos gift that cannot be revoked by codicil. The estate asserts the 2024 codicil revokes the 2022 conveyance, contending the 2022 deed contained a lifetime reservation of access making the gift conditional or incomplete. The probate court will determine which document is determinative at the May 18 hearing.

Hartwell holds physical possession of the Crimson Notebooks at the Reardon Manuscript Vault Bay 14, with reading room access only and physical removal restricted pending probate court determination. Legal ownership is disputed and has not been determined. Current possession is not equivalent to legal ownership; the catalog status note added April 30, 2026 explicitly distinguishes physical possession from the pending ownership determination.

Yes, they are consistent. The 2019 Deed of Gift's Schedule A specifically retained the Crimson Notebooks as item #11; they were not transferred in 2019 and remained in decedent's home. Photo PH-2020-067 corroborates that the notebooks remained on his home shelf after the 2019 intake. The 2022 Supplementary Deed of Gift (and corresponding intake receipt #ARC-2022-0247) transferred them, after which the shelf is photographed empty (PH-2022-181, April 21, 2022).
